seoTitle: Import JSON Files Into Power BI and Flatten Nested Data
metaDescription: Learn how to import JSON files into Power BI and flatten nested records into a clean table structure ready for visualization and analysis.
intro: JSON files often bury useful data inside nested objects and arrays, which makes them unusable in Power BI until you flatten them. This guide walks through importing a JSON file and transforming those nested records into a structured table you can actually build visuals on. It's written for analysts who've hit a wall trying to load JSON data straight into a report.

steps.heading: Import the JSON file
steps.body: Use Power BI's Get Data option to load your JSON source, which will initially appear as a table of records or nested objects.

steps.heading: Flatten the nested records
steps.body: Use Power Query's expand and transform tools to break nested objects and arrays out into their own columns and rows.

steps.heading: Review real-world applications
steps.body: Apply the flattened structure to a practical case, such as turning nested customer feedback data into a table ready for sentiment or trend analysis.

steps.heading: Follow best practices for data integrity
steps.body: Check that flattening hasn't duplicated or dropped records, especially when arrays expand into multiple rows per original entry.

steps.heading: Set up automated refreshes
steps.body: Once the transformation steps are in place, they'll reapply automatically each time the JSON source refreshes, keeping your workflow consistent.

faq.question: Why does my JSON data show up as a single column in Power BI?
faq.answer: Power BI initially loads JSON as a column of records or lists because the nested structure hasn't been expanded yet; you need to use the expand feature in Power Query to break it out.

faq.question: What happens to nested arrays when I flatten JSON data?
faq.answer: Expanding an array typically creates one row per array item, so a record with three nested items becomes three rows tied to the same parent data.

faq.question: Can I automate JSON flattening for recurring imports?
faq.answer: Yes, once you've set up the transformation steps in Power Query, they reapply automatically every time the file refreshes, as long as the JSON structure stays consistent.

faq.question: Does flattening JSON data risk losing information?
faq.answer: It shouldn't if done correctly, but it's worth spot-checking record counts before and after flattening to confirm nothing was accidentally dropped.
